The durable pattern is: create one universal source text, mirror its critical semantics into repo and runtime control files, validate those mirrors, and encode repeated-failure alarms plus end-of-task-only reporting so the directive cannot soften over time.
entries.impact: Codex now has a more durable sensor layer for compound improvement work, with less room for future sessions to forget that self-observation must be active, persistent, delayed until the end of the task, and escalated when the same failure repeats.

entries.summary: Codex now carries Directive 3 as a canonical universal bridge plus stronger repo/runtime mirrors and validator-backed resilient-bugfixing semantics.
entries.context_for_other_clis: This fix applies to the third-highest directive in the Codex environment itself. The user supplied a stronger universal Directive 3 text that defines resilient bugfixing as mandatory behavior for every environment bug in hooks, rules, settings, MCP usage, validation, scripts, agents, and skills. To make that directive durable, Codex needed a canonical bridge file, stronger mirrors in AGENTS, memory, rules, README, skill guidance, and validators that enforce the richer bugfix workflow.
entries.symptom_before_fix: Before this upgrade Codex already had resilient bugfixing as a dedicated directive, but it still lived mostly as a shorter process summary. There was no canonical universal Directive 3 bridge comparable to Directives 1 and 2, the repo/runtime mirror requirement was not explicit everywhere, and the validators did not assert stronger markers for the 5-step workflow, the full 8-point fix-induced-failure review, platform-aware durability rules, or the exact core sentence that an error should be made exactly once. That left room for future drift back to a softer interpretation.
entries.root_cause: The earlier Directive 3 upgrade stopped at a dedicated rule file and stronger ledger schema, but it did not yet create a canonical universal source plus mirror checks. Because the directive was still distributed across shorter paraphrases, later edits could preserve the general idea while weakening durability, platform, or mirror semantics without a clear validator failure.
entries.what_was_fixed: Added codex-setup/bridges/directive-3-resilient-bugfixing-universal.md as the canonical Directive 3 bridge, strengthened AGENTS, README, global rules, whiteboard memory, the dedicated resilient-bugfixing rule, the self-improve skill, and the resilient-bugfixing/workspace-scan references, redeployed the local self-improve skill, and expanded both validators to require the canonical bridge, the stronger wording, repo/runtime mirroring, the 5-step bugfix workflow, the full 8-point review, and the platform-specific durability markers.
entries.why_it_was_fixed: Directive 3 is the foundation below superintelligence and self-observation. If it can drift into a shallow bugfix checklist, Codex loses the process that is supposed to eliminate environment bugs permanently instead of relearning them after later sessions, updates, or platform changes.
entries.exact_error_text: Exact symptom text: Directive 3 existed, but without a canonical universal bridge and without validator-backed markers for repo/runtime mirroring, the 5-step bugfix workflow, the 8-point review, and platform rules, the stronger user intent could still erode over time.
entries.root_cause_why_chain: Why 1: the third directive still relied on distributed summaries instead of a canonical source. Why 2: without a canonical bridge, repo/runtime mirror expectations and stronger bugfix semantics were easier to weaken accidentally. Why 3: the validators still focused on the earlier directive-3 markers and did not yet assert the full richer contract end to end.
entries.related_error_sources_checked: Checked the drift surface across the canonical bridge directory, AGENTS, README, whiteboard memory, global rules, the dedicated resilient-bugfixing rule, the self-improve skill, the resilient-bugfixing and workspace-scan references, and both validators because all of them influence how Directive 3 is loaded, executed, and preserved over time.
entries.wrong_pattern_example: Wrong pattern: keep Directive 3 only as a shorter process summary spread across rule files and assume the richer user intent will survive later edits without a canonical bridge or validator-backed mirrors.
entries.right_pattern_example: Right pattern: define Directive 3 once as a canonical universal bridge, mirror its critical semantics into the controlling Codex files and the deployed runtime skill, and let both validators fail fast if the core sentence, workflow, review, platform, or mirror semantics drift.
entries.avoidance_rule: If a top-level bugfix directive controls every environment-level fix, then always keep one canonical source plus validator-backed mirrors in both repo and runtime paths instead of relying on loosely synchronized summaries.
entries.resilience_summary: The fix is defensive because it closes a semantic-drift path, survivable because Directive 3 now exists in both canonical and mirrored forms, extensible because later tooling can read the universal bridge directly, documented in both whiteboard and ledger, and damage-free because it hardens coordination rules rather than mutating project runtime state.
entries.fix_induced_failure_review: Dependencies: AGENTS, whiteboard memory, global rules, resilient-bugfixing rule, self-improve skill, reference files, the new bridge file, and both validators were reviewed together. Failure scenario: if a future edit weakens Directive 3, the validator now fails on missing bridge or marker coverage. State changes: guidance, mirrors, and validator rules only. Race conditions: none. Backward compatibility: existing resilient-bugfixing behavior remains but becomes more explicit and harder to erode. Platform effects: shared shell and PowerShell enforcement. Update resistance: repo/runtime mirrors and canonical bridge reduce silent drift. Graceful degradation: if any mirror weakens, validation fails instead of silently accepting a softer directive.
entries.verification: Verified by reinstalling the self-improve skill via both shell and PowerShell installers, then running bash codex-setup/scripts/validate-codex-setup.sh and pwsh -NoProfile -File codex-setup/scripts/validate-codex-setup.ps1; both validators passed on macOS after the Directive 3 hardening.
entries.portability_notes: Other CLI environments should treat resilient bugfixing as a canonical directive, not just a process note. The durable pattern is: create one universal source text, mirror its critical semantics into repo and runtime control files, validate those mirrors, and encode the 5-step workflow, the 8-point review, and platform-aware durability rules so the directive cannot soften over time.
entries.impact: Codex now has a more durable foundation for environment bugfixes, with less room for future sessions to forget that environment bugs must be fixed once, across related surfaces, across platforms, and with explicit future-failure review before they are considered complete.

entries.summary: The OpenAI-Docs MCP smoke now proves a fresh docs tool path and exits early instead of waiting for a full docs answer every time.
entries.context_for_other_clis: This fix belongs to the Codex-side validation path after the directive-3 sync rebase. A healthy fresh Codex exec started openaiDeveloperDocs, reached the docs tool call, and still failed the bounded smoke because no final AVAILABLE reply arrived before the budget.
entries.symptom_before_fix: After rebasing on top of the latest origin/main, pwsh validate-codex-setup.ps1 failed again although the fresh Codex exec showed mcp startup ready for openaiDeveloperDocs and logged a real openaiDeveloperDocs tool invocation. The validator either timed out at 90000 ms or spent the full budget waiting on a healthy but slow docs response.
entries.root_cause: The smoke script still treated a completed model reply as the only proof of availability. That conflated docs-answer latency with runtime health, even though fresh MCP startup plus a real openaiDeveloperDocs tool dispatch had already proven that the configured server and tool route were alive.
entries.what_was_fixed: check-openai-docs-mcp.mjs now uses a lighter list_openai_docs probe, watches fresh Codex exec output incrementally, and accepts either an explicit AVAILABLE reply or a confirmed docs-tool dispatch after healthy MCP startup.
entries.why_it_was_fixed: The validator should prove configuration and real tool reachability, not falsely fail or burn the full timeout on slow docs-answer latency.
entries.exact_error_text: Exact symptom text: Fresh Codex exec did not confirm openaiDeveloperDocs availability within the bounded retry window.
entries.root_cause_why_chain: Why 1: the validator failed although the fresh session started openaiDeveloperDocs correctly. Why 2: the old success rule waited for a final AVAILABLE reply instead of recognizing that the docs tool had already been dispatched. Why 3: that made a healthy runtime look broken whenever the first docs response was slower than the bounded budget.
entries.related_error_sources_checked: Checked the shared Node smoke script, both shell wrappers, and both full validators because the same docs-health assumption drives Bash and PowerShell. Also reran the standalone docs smoke directly so the fix was proven at the narrowest layer before retesting the larger validators.
entries.wrong_pattern_example: Wrong pattern: require a full docs answer from a fresh spawned agent even after healthy MCP startup and a real docs-tool dispatch already proved the runtime path is alive.
entries.right_pattern_example: Right pattern: prove docs health in layers with config inspection, docs URL reachability, fresh MCP startup, and either an explicit AVAILABLE reply or a confirmed openaiDeveloperDocs tool dispatch with a short grace window for immediate tool errors.
entries.avoidance_rule: If a fresh MCP smoke already proves startup and real tool dispatch, then validate that path directly and exit early instead of treating slow downstream content latency as a broken environment.
entries.resilience_summary: The fix is defensive because it keeps bounded retries and still fails when startup or tool dispatch never appears, faster because it exits as soon as the healthy docs path is proven, and update-resistant because Bash and PowerShell keep sharing one Node implementation instead of drifting into separate heuristics.
entries.fix_induced_failure_review: Dependencies: codex exec startup, the openaiDeveloperDocs MCP registration, docs URL reachability, stdout and stderr parsing, and the wrapper scripts were reviewed together. Failure scenario: if the MCP server is unknown, disabled, or never reaches a real tool call, validation still fails loudly. State change: none outside temporary smoke files. Race risk is bounded by the explicit 1500 ms grace window before early success. Backward compatibility remains because the wrapper interface and validator call sites stay unchanged. Platform effects improve because both macOS Bash and PowerShell now use the same incremental health proof. Graceful degradation is preserved because a real docs outage still aborts validation.
entries.verification: Verified with bash codex-setup/scripts/check-openai-docs-mcp.sh --timeout-ms 90000, bash codex-setup/scripts/validate-codex-setup.sh, and pwsh -NoProfile -File codex-setup/scripts/validate-codex-setup.ps1. All three completed successfully on this macOS host after the shared smoke-script hardening.
entries.portability_notes: Other CLIs that validate a fresh MCP-backed docs route should distinguish configuration and tool-dispatch health from the latency of the final content answer. Keep the runtime smoke bounded, add a short grace window for immediate tool errors, and share the heuristic in one cross-platform implementation if both Bash and PowerShell wrappers exist.
entries.impact: Docs-MCP validation is now both faster on healthy runs and less likely to report a false outage when the server is alive but the first docs answer is slow.

entries.summary: code-search now resolves the active pointer snapshot before the legacy index.db
entries.context_for_other_clis: This fix affects the local Codex code-search runtime and the direct health-check workflow. The MCP server had been reading the stale legacy database even though the active pointer in current.txt had already moved to index-17.db.
entries.symptom_before_fix: search_status reported /Users/frank/Codex/.code-search/index.db with 184 files and 800 chunks, while the active snapshot in current.txt was index-17.db with 673 files and 11053 chunks.
entries.root_cause: listDbCandidates in mcp-code-search/src/db-state.ts prioritized the legacy index.db before the pointer file, so both search_status and search_code could open the wrong database whenever both existed.
entries.why_it_was_fixed: make the live search runtime use the same active snapshot as the reindex pointer
entries.exact_error_text: search_status showed Database: /Users/frank/Codex/.code-search/index.db with 184 files and 800 chunks while the healthy workspace pointer was index-17.db with 673 files and 11053 chunks.
entries.root_cause_why_chain: Why 1: the MCP server opened the legacy database before the active pointer. Why 2: the candidate list still treated index.db as primary even when current.txt existed. Why 3: the status path and search path therefore drifted away from the reindex pointer and reported stale totals.
entries.related_error_sources_checked: Checked the same candidate-resolution surface in search_status, search_code, and the health-check script because they must all observe the same active snapshot and must not diverge between status and query paths.
entries.wrong_pattern_example: Wrong pattern: prefer the legacy index.db whenever it exists and only consult current.txt later.
entries.right_pattern_example: Right pattern: read current.txt first, then fall back to index.db only if the pointer is missing or invalid.
entries.avoidance_rule: If a code-search workspace uses pointer-based snapshots, then always resolve the pointer before any legacy database fallback and restart long-lived runtime processes after changing the resolver order.
entries.resilience_summary: The fix is defensive because it forces both search and status onto the same active snapshot, update-resilient because the pointer remains authoritative across reindexes, extensible because new candidate types can still be added after the pointer, documented in the ledger, and damage-free because the change only affects database selection.
entries.fix_induced_failure_review: Dependencies: search_code, search_status, the pointer file, and the reindex workflow all share the same resolver. Failure scenario: if the pointer is missing or malformed, the code still falls back to the legacy database instead of crashing. State changes: none beyond database selection. Race conditions: low, but the runtime cache should be restarted after changing the resolver order so long-lived sessions do not keep the stale store. Backward compatibility: legacy single-file indexes still open when no pointer exists. Platform effects: pointer selection works the same on macOS and Windows. Update resistance: future reindexes keep the pointer authoritative. Graceful degradation: missing or unreadable pointer files fall back cleanly to the old database.
entries.verification: Verified with the fresh code-search health smoke on /Users/frank/Codex: it now reports activeDb index-17.db, 673 files, and 11053 chunks, matching the workspace pointer instead of the stale legacy database.
entries.portability_notes: Any other CLI that uses pointer-based search snapshots should treat the pointer as the source of truth and only fall back to a legacy database when the pointer is absent. Long-lived runtime processes should be restarted after changing candidate priority so they do not hold stale open connections.
entries.impact: Search and status now resolve the same active database snapshot.

entries.summary: code-search now rebinds a stale cached store when the active snapshot changes
entries.context_for_other_clis: This fix affects the local Codex code-search MCP server during future sessions. A long-lived runtime can keep an open SQLite connection even after the active snapshot has moved or the loaded store has become stale, so the server needs an explicit guard before serving search requests.
entries.symptom_before_fix: A previously started code-search runtime could keep serving an old open store after the active pointer changed or the underlying DB path no longer matched the live snapshot, even though fresh sessions and the health check were already correct.
entries.root_cause: The MCP server cached the VectorStore across requests and relied on candidate order alone. That was sufficient for fresh startup paths, but a stale runtime could still hold a connection that should be discarded before the next query.
entries.why_it_was_fixed: make future sessions self-heal when the runtime and active snapshot drift apart
entries.exact_error_text: A long-lived code-search process could continue serving a cached store whose database path no longer matched the active snapshot, so a fresh rebinding step was needed before queries.
entries.root_cause_why_chain: Why 1: the server cached the open SQLite store between requests. Why 2: candidate order alone does not force a stale live connection to be dropped unless the runtime explicitly checks for drift. Why 3: without an explicit guard, a future session can keep serving the wrong store even though the on-disk snapshot has already advanced.
entries.related_error_sources_checked: Checked the same drift surface in search_code, search_status, cached store reuse, and the active-snapshot resolver because all of them can observe the same runtime-vs-snapshot mismatch.
entries.wrong_pattern_example: Wrong pattern: trust a cached DB connection forever once it has opened successfully.
entries.right_pattern_example: Right pattern: compare the cached path against the current active snapshot before each query and drop the connection when they diverge.
entries.avoidance_rule: If a code-search runtime caches a DB connection, then always compare it against the current active snapshot and reopen on mismatch before serving requests.
entries.resilience_summary: The fix is defensive because it drops stale connections before use, update-resilient because the guard runs whenever a request reaches the server, extensible because the same check can protect new query paths, documented in the ledger, and damage-free because it only changes store selection.
entries.fix_induced_failure_review: Dependencies: the cached VectorStore, the active snapshot resolver, and the request handlers were reviewed together. Failure scenario: if the guard itself fails, the server still falls back to the normal open-on-demand path. State changes: the fix only closes and reopens the store. Race conditions: low, but the guard runs before each request so drift is corrected early. Backward compatibility: healthy fresh sessions behave the same. Platform effects: the guard is platform-neutral. Update resistance: future snapshot switches and future long-lived sessions are both covered. Graceful degradation: if the preferred snapshot cannot be resolved, the server can still fall back to the best available database.
entries.verification: Verified by code review plus fresh health and smoke runs after the cached-store guard was added; the active snapshot is still reported as index-17.db and the code-search smoke query returns a real semantic hit.
entries.portability_notes: Any other CLI that keeps a cached search index open across requests should compare the cached path against the current active snapshot before reuse and close stale handles proactively.
entries.impact: Long-lived code-search runtimes now self-heal stale connections instead of serving them indefinitely.
